title: Travel & Accommodation
shortSummary: Check in to a hotel, ask about your stay, and handle travel basics in Korean.
culturalNote: Korea offers everything from international hotels to 게스트하우스 (geseuteuhauseu, guesthouses), 모텔 (motel), and traditional 한옥 (hanok) stays in restored wooden houses. Many budget travellers also use 찜질방 (jjimjilbang), 24-hour bathhouses with shared sleeping areas, which are a uniquely Korean experience. Service is famously attentive and polite, and staff will use formal speech with guests, so you will often hear the 합니다 style at a front desk. Check-in (체크인, chekeuin) and check-out (체크아웃, chekeuaut) use English loanwords, making them easy to recognise. Tipping is not customary in Korea and can even cause confusion, so a sincere 감사합니다 is the right way to show appreciation. When asking for help, leading with 실례합니다 (excuse me) and keeping to the polite -요 form will always be well received.

grammar.explanation: To put a verb in the past, add 았어요 if the last stem vowel is ㅏ or ㅗ, and 었어요 otherwise. 예약하다 (yeyakhada, to reserve) becomes 예약했어요 (yeyakhaesseoyo, I reserved).
grammar.commonMistake: Using the present 해요 when you mean a completed action; the past needs 했어요.

grammar.rule: Wanting to do something with -고 싶어요
grammar.explanation: To say you want to do an action, attach 고 싶어요 (go sipeoyo) to a verb stem. 체크인하다 (chekeuinhada) becomes 체크인하고 싶어요 (I want to check in).

examples.en: I want to go to Korea.

grammar.commonMistake: Using 고 싶어요 to describe wanting a noun; for nouns use 을/를 + 원해요 or 갖고 싶어요 instead.

dialogue.setup: Mia arrives at a guesthouse front desk to check in.

lines.speaker: 직원
lines.english: Yes, what is your name, please?

lines.speaker: 미아
lines.english: It's Mia. I'd like to check in.

lines.speaker: 직원
lines.english: Confirmed. The Wi-Fi password is here.

lines.speaker: 직원
lines.english: Yes, it's included. Check-out is at eleven.

lines.speaker: 미아
lines.english: I understand. Thank you very much!

quiz.question: How do you say 'I made a reservation' in Korean?
quiz.explanation: 예약했어요 (yeyakhaesseoyo) is the past tense of 예약하다, 'to reserve'.

quiz.question: Which phrase means 'I'd like to check in'?
quiz.explanation: 체크인 하고 싶어요 uses -고 싶어요 to express wanting to check in.

quiz.question: 쉬___ 싶어요. (want to rest)
quiz.explanation: 쉬고 싶어요 (swigo sipeoyo) means 'I want to rest'; the pattern is verb stem + 고 싶어요.

quiz.question: What custom around tipping applies in Korea?
quiz.explanation: Tipping is not part of Korean culture; a polite 감사합니다 is the right thank-you.

quiz.question: Arrange: 얼마예요 / 하룻밤에 (how much per night)
quiz.explanation: 하룻밤에 얼마예요? (harutbame eolmayeyo) asks the price per night.
